text: Irradiation of smoldering biomass smoke in a large outdoor photoreactor Photochemical Aging of Smoldering Biomass Smoke Aging of Wood Smoke in a Dual Outdoor Photoreactor In-situ studies of photochemical aging of biomass smoke under realistic atmospheric conditions provide a crucial link between biomass burning emissions and their subsequent effects on climate forcing. Here we investigate the photochemical aging of biomass smoke from smoldering wood burns using a 104 m 3 dual outdoor chamber under solar irradiation over a wide range of relative humidity (RH) and NO x conditions. Bio-mass smoke color evolution and resulting light absorption of organic carbon (OC) measured using a UV-Vis spectrometer equipped with an integrating sphere was measured as a function of time and conditions. In-situ measurements reveal that the high RH leads to faster color decay of the aerosol, while small enhancements in aerosol color stability by up to a factor of 1.05 is observed at high NO x levels. In general, the mass absorption cross-section (MAC) of OC (integrated over 280–600 nm) was found to increase by 11–54% during the morning photochemical processes (except for the case of high RH) and then decrease by 19–68% in the afternoon. We carried out multi-spectroscopic studies to probe in detail the detailed evolution of biomass smoke photochemistry, elucidating the relationship between the chemistry and aerosol optical properties. Rapid photochemical processes resulted in fast bleach-out of PAH fluorescence signal and declining levoglucosan concentrations, confirming the ongoing depletion of primary organic aerosol (OA). Moreover, the aerosol became less hygroscopic over time based on the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. Similar trends were observed from the analysis of field data from ambient smoke from the 2012 County Line wildfire in Florida. Thus, biomass-burning OC leads to a net decrease in light absorption after around 8–9 hours of photochemical aging under sunlight conditions.
